context.description: The page develops an analogy between dual awareness and lucid dreaming, arguing that both reveal the dreamlike character of consciousness while still binding the mind to the rules of the current state. It then examines why the moment of falling asleep is never remembered and uses that gap to question whether a higher self could be consciously known after returning. The passage ends by asking whether dreams can reveal more about this limitation.
normalization_notes: Mapped generic publication/book hint to nonfiction philosophy and philosophy of consciousness.
Translated German running prose into English control-language summary.

rows.text_content: Dual awareness is compared with lucid dreaming, showing that both expose the dreamlike character of consciousness while still forcing decisions to follow the rules of the current state.
The text argues that the moment of falling asleep cannot be remembered, unlike waking, because that transition is defined by forgetting and erases the waking self from the dream self.
It then raises the possibility of a higher self and asks whether dreams may reveal more about that limitation.
